title: Agent of change: PM’s about-turn on AI laws
full_text:
Anthony Albanese has delivered a U-turn on artificial intelligence regulation by vowing to develop a world-leading framework of laws specifically targeting the technology, prompting business to warn against designing standards that are out of line with international competitors.
The Prime Minister used a major speech on Wednesday to unveil plans to create a legal structure establishing “Australian standards for AI” that would be legislated early next year, despite last year rejecting the need for a standalone AI act.
He said he would seek the support from state and territory leaders at a national cabinet meeting later this month for a framework for data centres and copyright rules that would be the first legislation of its kind worldwide.
Under pressure from both unions and One Nation tapping into public concerns about the rollout of AI, Mr Albanese said there was an active role for government to ensure the technology did not undermine “our national values and our national interests”.
Included in the framework will be laws requiring data centre developers to underwrite their own energy supplies, reduce power when the grid is under strain and use water efficiently.
Mr Albanese said clear rules for data centre operators would “enhance our appeal to international investors” and provide for faster approvals. He also vowed to deliver the “strongest copyright protections for Australian artists is for Australia to be active and involved”. “No company should use Australian books, music, art or news to build or train AI without the artist’s control,” Mr Albanese said in Sydney. “Our laws will spell that out, plain as day.” The Australian revealed this month Labor was preparing to endorse a new approach to AI at next week’s national conference, with the draft policy platform being heavily amended to include a raft of union demands to regulate the technology.
The government will also use a separate process to bolster worker and consumer protections in relation to AI, with the ACTU backing Labor’s push to take “a more active role in AI policy”.
Unions are expected to push for further AI protections at the national conference, including demands workers are consulted on the rollout of the technology in the workplace. The government will develop legislation with advice from a new AI section set up in the Prime Minister’s office, with tech companies vowing to work constructively with Labor as the standards are designed.
Last year, the government rejected the need for specific AI laws as pushed for by former industry minister Ed Husic. The National AI Plan, launched in December by Industry Minister Tim Ayres and Assistant Technology Minister Andrew Charlton, instead tasked a new advisory body to over time consider “gaps” in existing regulations exposed through AI. While the government’s proposal is not as far reaching as a European-style AI act encompassing economy-wide rules, enforcing data centre expectations by law is a more heavy-handed approach.
The Business Council of Australia and the Australian Chamber of Commerce and Industry did not immediately criticise the government’s changed approach, in contrast to the fierce criticism Labor received from corporate leaders after handing down tax hikes in the May budget.
But BCA chief executive Bran Black and ACCCI chief executive Andrew McKellar warned the government against being too heavy-handed when developing the national standards.
“Australia already has way too much regulation,” Mr Black said. “We know that when large technology companies are looking at places to invest, certainly they do see Australia as having lots of advantages.
“But if we overregulate, we risk losing the benefit of those advantages. We’d like to see a standard that applies as closely as possible with respect to the way in which companies operate at an international level.” Microsoft, one of the world’s largest tech companies, was effusive about Mr Albanese’s speech, saying it was a “clarion call” and that it set a “confident direction”.
Billionaire tech boss Robin Khuda held a private meeting with Mr Albanese after the Prime Minister’s speech in Sydney and offered qualified backing.
“Industry is ready to invest, but Australia must continue improving the speed, efficiency, and competitiveness of infrastructure delivery if we want to attract global capital and become a leading AI nation,” Mr Khuda told The Australian. Microsoft Australia president Jane Livesey said she welcomed “the government’s sharpened focus for all who share responsibility in Australia’s AI transformation”.
Ms Livesey said the Prime Minister’s address laid out a “confident direction for how we must calibrate national policy to ensure the benefits of this transformational technology are shared widely and equitably – an ambition Microsoft strongly supports”.
Google hailed Mr Albanese’s “constructive” policy-setting speech. “Google is committed to doing AI the right way through our industry-leading approach to water and energy use, skilling and training to deliver the technology infrastructure that will secure and support the future of local, national, and global economies,” a spokesperson said.
An OpenAI spokeswoman said the company would engage “constructively throughout this process with the government, creators and industry on an approach that is practical, sustainable and right for Australia”.
Anthropic’s general counsel, Jeff Bleich – formerly US ambassador to Australia and Obama White House special counsel – said the company “respect(s) the process articulated by the Prime Minister today for establishing Australia’s AI framework”.
Amazon, the largest operator of data centres in the world and itself a major AI developer, said it was “pleased to see the government’s acknowledgment of the essential role data centres have in shaping a prosperous future for Australia”.
News Corp Australasia chairman Michael Miller, the publisher of The Australian, said Mr Albanese must now “move fast and deliver” after laying out his vision.
ACTU secretary Sally McManus said “Australians don’t trust AI because of the lack of clear benefit for working people”. “We have had big promises, but so far, no guarantee that any benefits will be shared with workers,” she said.
“Unions don’t want to veto technology use, only to veto the harm it could cause.” Talking up his support for AI adoption, Mr Albanese said it was not the government’s “goal to try and legislate for every possible eventuality or risk”.
“That only creates a risk of Australia missing out on investment altogether,” he said.
“This is about having the flexibility to keep pace with change and get out in front of it.
“This is about building Australians’ confidence and trust in AI and our nation’s capacity to manage it, ensuring that our national interests and our national security are protected and providing the certainty for growth, for jobs and for investment.” Mr Albanese said the government was consulting on whether further regulations were needed to address AI’s impact on schools, children, defence and national security. “We should not treat AI as a threat to good jobs – we must use it as an instrument to help create them,” he said.
ALBANESE’S AI CHALLENGE THE NATION The choice that we have is how we apply our enduring values of fairness and opportunity to make this technology work for us, for workers and communities, for our economy and our environment
JOBS We cannot settle for a short-term boom in capital expenditure and construction. We must create a new generation of good, secure jobs for our economy
WATER AND POWER
We will create a legal obligation for the next generation of large-scale data centres to underwrite new power supply. To pay their full share of grid connection, so no costs are passed on to homes or businesses
COPYRIGHT Australian writers, musicians, artists and journalists must retain ownership and control of their work. Our laws will spell that out, plain as day
